name: Flip Method (Clock)
description: The Flip method is a beginner-friendly Clock solving technique. It solves the front cross first, then the front corners, flips the puzzle, and solves the entire back side. This approach completes the front face fully before moving to the back, providing a straightforward and intuitive solving experience.

steps.name: Front Cross
steps.description: Solve the four edge clocks on the front face to form a cross pattern. Use appropriate pin configurations to isolate and set each edge clock to 12 o'clock.
tips: Develop a consistent solving order for the four edges
Press pins carefully to avoid affecting non-target clocks
Plan which edge to solve first based on the scramble

steps.name: Front Corners
steps.description: Solve the four corner clocks on the front face. Since the front cross is already solved, use pin configurations that adjust corners without disturbing the edges.
tips: Learn which pin combinations isolate corner clocks on the front
Be mindful that corner moves can affect the back face
Solve corners adjacent to already-correct edges when possible

steps.name: Flip
steps.description: Flip the Clock puzzle over to access the back face. The front face should be fully solved at this point.
tips: Flip quickly with a consistent grip
The front face is complete — now focus entirely on the back
Use the transition moment to mentally plan the back side

steps.name: Solve Back Side
steps.description: Solve all clocks on the back face — both edges and corners. Use pin configurations that preserve the solved front face while correcting the back.
tips: Understand how back-face moves interact with the front face
Use pins to isolate back-face clocks as much as possible
Practice this step extensively since it determines final solving speed

pros: Very intuitive — solve one face completely before the other
Easy to learn for Clock beginners
Good starting method before learning no-flip techniques
cons: Flip adds time to every solve
Back side solving can be constrained by front face preservation
Limited optimization compared to simultaneous methods
